data.epi_content:
When was it?

Perhaps it was when I had just entered elementary school and played soccer with the other children.

Everyone fears being ostracized, I suppose, but I was particularly terrified of it.

So I would cling to the kids who were good at both sports and studies, constantly flattering them.

Rather than flattery, maybe I was more like a little bell that rang with praise beside them.

I quit doing that after a while when I realized how pointless it was.

The reason I stopped was probably because my body had suddenly grown taller than that child.

I've never flattered anyone in my life.

Born perfect from the start, why would I ever need to flatter anyone?

And after some time passed, that child I used to flatter had become smaller, and started doing to me exactly what I had done to them.

From then on, I think I started treating everyone more or less the same.

"Ah."

I shake my head slightly to clear these useless thoughts and get up from the bed.

Recalling old memories gives me a throbbing headache.

Sometimes, I see images that aren't me but someone else.

For example, visions of myself—no, a little girl—running around happily with a teddy bear received from a gloomy middle-aged man with a mustache.

I look in the mirror.

As I make a slight frown at my reflection, the girl in the mirror frowns back.

I made a sad face.

Next, I smile.

The girl mimics with a bizarrely unnatural smile.

Unnaturally enough to give me chills.

Because I don't really know how to smile.

When have I ever had reason to smile?

My snow-white hair, as if bleached and boiled, cascades down my shoulders to my waist.

My slightly cold eyes give the impression of being completely dried out.

It hurts a little when you put it that way.

Ahaha.

"Huff."

I close my eyes briefly, open them again, and sigh.

Considering the tantrum I threw in my room yesterday, someone must have come by.

Probably that nameless maid.

The poor unfortunate soul who earned the misfortune of cleaning my room while grumbling irritably.

I'm glad I hid the pistol in the drawer before going to bed.

Father gave it to me as a gift for self-defense, decorated with gold ornaments and whatnot, but a gun is still a gun.

They would confiscate it, probably.

The mirror that should be shattered is perfectly intact.

I had thrown a coffee cup at the wall, but the black stain is nowhere to be seen—everything is pristine white.

Magic, right.

You're probably not familiar with magic.

My body doesn't feel grimy at all; I'm even wearing a clean school uniform.

I seem to be alone, and yet...

I always feel like someone is watching and monitoring me.

That fact is extremely unpleasant.

I feel the same way.

I've always lived under various gazes—obvious stares, stares trying to hide, admiring glances, hateful glares, greedy looks.

Who would have thought?

Entering a novel must be a more common occurrence than I imagined.

Even someone like me, who was just reading a romance novel, could become a little miss with such a petty temperament.

...
data.epi_content2:
Why did I have to enter this particular novel?

Why did I have to become this particular child?

Why did I have to...

Let's stop.

Repeating negative thoughts in my head is good for escaping reality, but at some point, it becomes difficult to run away.

To change my train of thought, I look around my now-clean room.

I'd rather have tea than coffee.

"I want to drink oolong tea with lots of ice."

I remember the iced oolong tea for 2,000 won they sold at a café five minutes from home.

I liked coffee too, but I often bought that tea because of its unique aroma.

It was affordable too.

All that's available is coffee.

What's wrong with coffee?

The beans might be of lower quality, but I can't get anything better than this.

I take out the finely ground powder from the shelf and place an appropriate amount on the paper filter.

I place the filter over the cup and, surprisingly, I know how to use magic to pour hot water from my fingertips.

If only I had something like instant ramen, that would have been even better.

No need to boil water or light a fire—it would have been perfect.

The coffee aroma fills the room.

It covers the faint perfume scent and the warm body scent—what you might call a personal fragrance.

With only the coffee aroma filling the space, I feel at ease.

After brewing a strong cup, I throw the coffee grounds into the trash.

Some splattered on the floor, but someone will clean it up anyway.

It feels like slowly sinking into a swamp that someone is carefully maintaining.

There's no sticky feeling or the peculiar grainy sensation when touching mud, but very slowly...

This young lady—no, me.

Well, it doesn't matter since it's me now.

Anyway, this young lady really liked a handsome man.

She was so jealous that she would harass and drive away any woman who approached him, especially if that woman was his childhood friend.

I don't know.

It was something like a first love, I suppose.

Whether it was truly love or just possessiveness, I'm not sure.

But being as immature as her age, despite her jealousy, she didn't properly bully others. Instead, she used inefficient, irrational, and unnecessary methods that damaged her own reputation and alienated her allies.

Just enough to give the protagonist minor emotional wounds without serious injury, making readers feel angry.

You know, the protagonist crying alone on a bench or in a corner, then coincidentally meeting the male lead.

Then falling into his arms, shedding a few tears while explaining what happened, and watching the male lead vow to get even—though "revenge" might be a bit dramatic. It's quite an interesting scene.

For that one scene, I as a character will be completely destroyed.

She tried quite hard to create that scene.

Taking her followers to say harsh words, telling the Duke's son not to flirt with the protagonist, and then getting stupidly angry when told it wasn't her business.

When the protagonist approached wanting to reconcile, she rudely turned her back and left. When the protagonist still followed and grabbed her hand, she slapped her face.

What could I do? It wasn't that I hated the girl so much, but I didn't like what she was doing.

The victim of the bullying was naturally the protagonist, and the villain who torments the protagonist deserves to die.

To think that insignificant girl was the protagonist—then was I, who lived my whole life being revered, merely a supporting character from the beginning?

Perhaps closer to a tool with a predetermined purpose than a supporting character. Hee.

Usually, the clumsy and cruel bullying of childhood ends with the perpetrator venting their feelings, while the victim ends up in tatters.

Poetic justice in reality is appealing but rarely happens.

But this isn't reality—it's a novel.

And most novels take the form of poetic justice.

Readers, whether they prefer female-oriented or male-oriented stories, mostly want to see villains fall miserably.

There are far more readers who identify with the protagonist than with the villain.
data.epi_content3:
How could I have known I was living in a novel?

You've probably never considered that the world you live in might be a novel too.

I didn't even know I was the villain.

I was born to live this way my entire life.

Ah, I've finished my coffee.

That was the last of the grounds, I think.

I don't feel like going out.

"Well, that's just an excuse."

I don't know about the coffee, but the cups seem to be provided by the Academy—they replace them with new ones no matter how many I break.

So I just let it fall weakly from my hand.

Crash.

It didn't make a particularly dramatic sound.

It just split in half.

When I woke up, there was a small cup and a bottle filled with numerous pills beside me.

Originally, this body should have been slowly dying in agony after consuming poison, though not a proper deadly one.

But instead of dying, I woke up.

I don't think anything will change because of that.

Usually in most novels where someone possesses a villainess, the possession happens before her downfall.

Or even if something happens, they present a way to salvage the situation.

My backing is gone.

Sadly, the family has fallen.

I'll probably be sold off somewhere.

And it's not like I'm particularly exceptional myself.

But at least I'm beautiful, aren't I?

And now I've become the target of clumsy and cruel bullying.

Even if someone messes with me, there's not much to fear anymore.

The higher you are, the harder you fall.

And during that fall, this girl covered me with her corpse and ran away somewhere.

Or maybe she became one with me.

The strangely feminine change in my speech pattern, and when I recall memories—this young lady's memories, no, my memories... ah.

Anyway. Anyway. Anyway.

That's just how the story goes.

Though the family has fallen, it seems I haven't been expelled from the Academy since the tuition was paid in advance.

I thought that was fortunate, but instead of becoming no different from a commoner, I've become someone the commoners bully.

When I went to class after waking up in this body, someone shoved my shoulder as they passed by. My desk was covered with degrading graffiti, and because of violence I don't even want to mention, I've been holed up in my room for days.

They said they couldn't stand how arrogantly I used to behave. Haha.

The world may be merciful to protagonists, but not so much to disposable villains.

When I hear the clicking sounds at night of someone trying to pick my door lock, possibly to attack me, I can't help but cry in fear.

Let's set aside these dark stories and talk about family.

I don't know how Father died, but the letter said it was an accidental death—he must have committed suicide. As for Mother... I'm not sure.

In the end, there's not much to do to pass the time in this small room I've escaped to.

On the first day, after being locked in the closet all day and returning to my room feeling depressed, I did some obscene things like putting something black and hard in my mouth and taking it out, or playing that roulette game Russians enjoy for a bit of thrill.

Knock, knock, knock.

Someone is knocking on the door.

It's probably the protagonist.

She's so kind that she worries even about the person who bullied her.

Though "bullied" might be too strong a word—it was rather cute. Well, it's a novel after all.

Now it's reality.
